patches_reviewed.subject: Re: Deleted snapshots stay in squota, mayube because of bees?
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue where deleted snapshots remain in the quota system due to a problem with assigning ownership of data blocks. The proposal is to add an ioctl that allows userspace to assign extents or files to a specific subvolume, rather than having the kernel make arbitrary decisions. This approach aims to avoid lossy processes and potential inconsistencies in metadata ownership.

review_comments.author: Marc MERLIN
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that in cases where a block is owned by multiple subvols, it's acceptable to assign the block to the subvol with the lower number, and suggested this as a simple solution for cleaning up unowned blocks.

Reviewer suggested a simple solution to resolve ownership of data blocks: picking the lowest ID subvolume as owner, which would be sufficient for this patch despite not being perfect

The reviewer, Marc MERLIN, expressed frustration with the current state of squotas in Btrfs, where deleted snapshots remain and cause clutter in the quota display. He acknowledged that perfect accounting is not possible due to shared blocks, but wants a simple solution to assign ownership of unowned extents to the remaining subvolumes.
review_comments.raw_body:
In the case of blocks that are owned by multiple subvols, no need to be
fancy, it's fine to just assign them to the subvol with the lower number
and be done with it.
The main idea being to clean up unowned blocks and the state I just
reported (see end of this mail), which I can reproduce pretty much at
will (start btrfs receive, let it run, have bees run at the same time,
kill btrfs receive, delete subvoll, and up with unowned blocks forever).

---

This doesn't need to be perfect, nor can you guess who to assign if
there are multiple others, just pick the lowest id one, and that's fine.

---

So let me give my outside view and opinion :)
s in squotas stands both for simple and sloppy :)
When I took them, I accepted that in the name of performance. 
"good enough" that I can live with performance wise, 1000x beats
"perfect but I can't deal with the performance hit"

I already accepted that accounting won't be perfect and it's not true
reality with shared blocks, never mind snapshots of snapshots, but even
more so with bees. I'm ok with that.
I just want to know that this 10 million file subvol is around 7.2TB
without spending hours and hours running a du -sh on it.
And let's be honest, even with du -sh, shared blocks can be an issue, so
really it's not really worse.

But I do want to fix this is very very vexing to see forever unless I
wipe the filesystem and start over:

Qgroupid    Referenced    Exclusive   Path 
--------    ----------    ---------   ---- 
0/5            4.78GiB      4.78GiB   <toplevel>
0/256         16.00KiB     16.00KiB   backup
0/258         16.00KiB     16.00KiB   backup-btrfssend
0/260          1.06GiB      1.06GiB   .beeshome
0/261         63.74GiB     63.74GiB   backup/win_kodi_ro.20260328_21:48:59
0/262        229.51GiB    229.51GiB   backup/ubuntu_kodi_ro.20260328_10:19:47
0/263        297.04MiB    297.04MiB   <squota space holder>   <-- be gone
0/264        187.38GiB    187.38GiB   backup/0Notmachines_kodi_ro.20260325_20:08:49
0/266         10.37GiB     10.37GiB   backup/1Appliances_kodi_ro.20260325_23:22:32
0/268        294.27GiB    294.27GiB   backup/debian32_kodi_ro.20260325_23:43:06
0/269        287.84MiB    287.84MiB   <squota space holder>a <-- be gone too ;)

I think it's pretty obvious where to assign them, it's the other
subvolume that didn't get deleted, and if there are 4 that these blocks
are shared against, any of the 4 will do, I truly don't care :)

hope that makes sense.

Marc
-- 
"A mouse is a device used to point at the xterm you want to type in" - A.S.R.
 
Home page: http://marc.merlins.org/                       | PGP 7F55D5F27AAF9D08

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH RFC v3 01/19] mm: thread user_addr through page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to thread user addresses through the page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing, but raises concerns about increasing the buddy surface and potential maintenance issues.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about increasing the buddy surface and potential maintenance issues, suggesting a more targeted approach to updating interfaces.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Apr 24, 2026 at 09:28:36AM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> On 4/23/26 16:57, Gregory Price wrote:
> > On Thu, Apr 23, 2026 at 04:13:50PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> >> On 4/23/26 15:42, Gregory Price wrote:
> >>
> >> Maybe we could forward the vma+addr here and call a vma_alloc_froze_folio() if
> >> we have a VMA+addr to have a clean interface.
> >>
> >> But really, that hugetlb code is rather messy. I'd vote for leaving hugetlb
> >> alone on a v1, and focusing on non-hugetlb first.
> >>
> > 
> > If we're ok increasing the buddy surface this way, then I'd vote for
> > only updating the exact interfaces that MST needs to update for his use
> > case in a base set of patches, and then have each additional updated
> > location (or logical set of locations) updated in follow-ups.
> > 
> > My initial go around with this - the patch was hard to read at best.
> > 
> > But I also think we should also seriously consider not increasing the
> > surface of the buddy. 
> 
> Exactly, that's why I am saying that vma_alloc_folio() is the only external
> interface people should be using with a user address. all other _noprof helpers
> are supposed to be internal.
> 
> For hugetlb, we might need another interface for frozen folios later, which is
> why I suggest to defer that.
> 

Yeah I follow now - we're of the same mind on all this then.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Clarified the intended interface for user addresses and suggested deferring additional interfaces until later.

review_comments.author: Michael S. Tsirkin
review_comments.summary: Indicated that an updated version of the patch (v4) is expected.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH RFC v3 01/19] mm: thread user_addr through page allocator for cache-friendly zeroing
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch proposes a change to how the page allocator handles zeroing of memory, specifically for cache-friendly zeroing. It involves passing user addresses through the page allocator and using a wrapper function to handle cacheline alignment on the way out. However, after exploring this approach, it was deemed that the increased complexity may not be worth the benefits.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) suggested simplifying the patch by making post_alloc_hook() a wrapper around a variant that consumes an address, rather than changing all callers to pass USER_ADDR_NONE, and requested keeping changes mostly within mm/page_alloc.c to minimize churn outside of this file.
review_comments.raw_body:
In v2 I commented [1]

"
For example, instead of changing all callers of post_alloc_hook() to
pass USER_ADDR_NONE, can we make post_alloc_hook() a simple wrapper
around a variant that consumes an address.

So isn't there a way we can just keep the changes mostly to mm/page_alloc.c?
"

That should avoid most of the churn outside of page_alloc, no?

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/r/4bdc66f2-1469-4b91-9935-74c3d3ca0ed9@kernel.org

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Michael Tsirkin
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Tsirkin expressed concerns about the proposed patch, citing increased complexity and potential performance issues due to longer function names and additional boilerplate code. He also noted that the changes would be harder to review and might lead to subtle slowdowns or corruption on non-x86 architectures.

Reviewer noted that adding a wrapper to pass __GFP_ZERO and post_alloc_hook() would simplify the interface but increase surface area in the buddy, and questioned whether the added complexity is worth it.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes I'm sorry I missed that one and didn't answer it. My bad.

To answer the question, no, definitely not *most*. Here are some numbers:

What we save: 11 one-line changes adding , USER_ADDR_NONE in callers
that don't care about user_addr (compaction, filemap, khugepaged,
migrate, page_frag_cache, shmem, slub, swap_state).

Changes we have to add: 8 changes
Rename 4 existing APIs adding _user: __alloc_pages,
__folio_alloc, folio_alloc_mpol, __alloc_frozen_pages + add
4 wrapper macros/inlines in gfp.h that forward to the _user variants with
USER_ADDR_NONE. Roughly 6-8 lines of boilerplate per API.

 
What stays the same, but now has to call the new APIs:

all internal plumbing (page_alloc.c, mempolicy.c, internal.h, hugetlb.c,
the actual user-page callers in memory.c/huge_memory.c/highmem.h):
38 calls, of these 21 in page_alloc.c, 17 outside it.


The changes would be less trivial to review, too. And I would not call
the resulting very long names "elegant".

More importantly, it makes it harder to review: instead of
compiler checking we did not forget a parameter, it compiles fine,
but we get a subtle slowdown or corruption on non x86.

And I thought you agreed with this sentiment when you said
"yea something that is harder to mess up would be nice":
https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260414062524-mutt-send-email-mst@kernel.org/

---

wrapper APIs, for no benefit.

But then, I'm not the maintainer here. And adding extra wrappers
is easy for me. So, let me know. Thanks!


-- 
MST

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that adding two paths into the buddy would increase surface area, but believes the performance gain is worth it and sees no elegant fix to avoid this.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch's approach to zeroing and cache alignment could be simplified by leveraging existing functionality in post_alloc_hook(), but expressed concern about added complexity to the buddy allocator

Reviewer David (Arm) noted that the patch could simplify the interface by adding wrappers to existing functions such as folio_alloc_mpol(), __alloc_frozen_pages_noprof(), and __alloc_pages() instead of introducing new code, but suggested leaving the hugetlb code untouched for a v1 release.
review_comments.raw_body:
We probably wouldn't need prof stuff if all we care about is vma_alloc_folio()
that will only be calling _noprof internally.

---

Right.

Looking at the patch, there would already be less churn if

folio_alloc_mpol()
__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof()
__alloc_pages()
post_alloc_hook()

Would have simple wrappers.

hugetlb calls  __alloc_frozen_pages(). That's rather nasty, given that
it wants to allocate a frozen folio.

alloc_hugetlb_folio() already consumes vma+addr.
alloc_buddy_hugetlb_folio_with_mpol() already consumes vma+addr.

Maybe we could forward the vma+addr here and call a vma_alloc_froze_folio() if
we have a VMA+addr to have a clean interface.

But really, that hugetlb code is rather messy. I'd vote for leaving hugetlb
alone on a v1, and focusing on non-hugetlb first.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Michael Tsirkin
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Michael Tsirkin expressed concern that the patch introduces non-orthogonal behavior by providing an optimization only for THP (Transparent Huge Pages), whereas users expect similar performance and predictability from hugetlb, which is harder to use but uses more resources.
review_comments.raw_body:
I just dislike it when things are non orthogonal.
People are used to: hugetlb = same perf as THP but more predictable at the cost
of being harder to use and using more resources.
Here, suddenly, we have an optimization but only for THP.

But sure, we can merge a part of it first.

-- 
MST

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that increasing the buddy surface might not be worth it and suggests a compromise: updating only the interfaces needed by MST in an initial set of patches, while considering alternatives to increase the surface.
review_comments.raw_body:
If we're ok increasing the buddy surface this way, then I'd vote for
only updating the exact interfaces that MST needs to update for his use
case in a base set of patches, and then have each additional updated
location (or logical set of locations) updated in follow-ups.

My initial go around with this - the patch was hard to read at best.

But I also think we should also seriously consider not increasing the
surface of the buddy.  We already have two patterns (either you need to
call folio_zero_user() or you don't) and adding the wrappers to handle
this internally means we have 3.

It's a nice to have, but i'm not sure it decreases maintenance issues.

Just my 2-cents for what it's worth.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that user_alloc_needs_zeroing() is not used with hugetlb, and suggested addressing this issue separately to clean up the hugetlb part more cleanly.
review_comments.raw_body:
Note that we also didn't care about user_alloc_needs_zeroing() with hugetlb so far.

It's not that it cannot be done with hugetlb, it's just a question about how it
can be done more cleanly; and that requires more work to clean the hugetlb part up.

Best done separately.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) pointed out that vma_alloc_folio() should be the only external interface used with a user address, and suggested deferring the addition of another interface for frozen folios until it's needed for hugetlb
review_comments.raw_body:
Exactly, that's why I am saying that vma_alloc_folio() is the only external
interface people should be using with a user address. all other _noprof helpers
are supposed to be internal.

For hugetlb, we might need another interface for frozen folios later, which is
why I suggest to defer that.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that he understands the reviewer's point about passing __GFP_ZERO and post_alloc_hook() handling zeroing, but still sees value in adding two paths into the buddy for cache-friendly zeroing.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah I follow now - we're of the same mind on all this then.

~Gregory

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v8 0/2] firmware_loader: allow firmware_class.path to take multiple paths

version_history.subject: [PATCH v8 0/2] firmware_loader: allow firmware_class.path to take multiple paths

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 1/2] firmware_loader: add search_path= module option for multi-path firmware lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new module option called firmware_class.search_path that allows users to specify multiple paths for firmware lookup. The input is preprocessed at boot or sysfs write time into a NUL-separated sequence of paths, avoiding per-load parsing overhead. The firmware lookup order is first the legacy path specified by firmware_class.path, then the colon-separated paths from search_path, and finally built-in default paths. Backslash escapes are supported for literal ':' and '\

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 2/2] selftests/firmware: add search path test for firmware_class.search_path=
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new selftest, fw_search.sh, to validate the firmware_class.search_path= module parameter. The test covers various scenarios including prioritized path ordering, empty paths, and handling of special characters in directory names. It also tests the interaction between search_path= and path= parameters. The test creates temporary directories for firmware and restores original values after each test case.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series allows the firmware_class.path to take multiple paths, enabling more flexible firmware lookup. The changes include adding a new search_path= module option and updating selftests to test this functionality.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The author is seeking approval from Greg to merge the patch into -next, indicating a desire for it to be included in v7.2.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 2026-04-23 at 14:16 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> This version just has a couple of very minor nits fixed that sashiko
> flagged. I'd love to see this go into -next soon, so it can make v7.2.
> 
> Greg, are you ok with this version?
> 
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
> Changes in v8:
> - add newline to output in fw_search_get()
> - fix test #3 to test non-present file properly
> - Link to v7: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260402-fw-path-v7-0-5b7e9c41a8c0@kernel.org
> 
> Changes in v7:
> - re-escape emitted paths in fw_search_get
> - fix potential out-of-bounds read in fw_get_filesystem_firmware()
> - ensure all files are cleaned up after failed selftests
> - Link to v6: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260401-fw-path-v6-0-4ebe70441839@kernel.org
> 
> Changes in v6:
> - Add fw_search_unescape() to make the search path parsing more readable
> - Rename search= to search_path=
> - Link to v5: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260323-fw-path-v5-0-e88b2fe145f3@kernel.org
> 
> Changes in v5:
> - Increase search= string length from 256 to 4096
> - Preprocess search= path when it's set rather than on every firmware load
> - Add selftests for search= functionality
> - Link to v4: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260320-fw-path-v4-1-7547e2f0dc64@kernel.org
> 
> Changes in v4:
> - Move search path to new search= option that is tried after path=
> - Link to v3: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260318-fw-path-v3-1-a701a08bc025@kernel.org
> 
> Changes in v3:
> - Allow '\' to escape a literal ':' or '\' in the string
> - Link to v2: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260318-fw-path-v2-1-8a106eb91eb4@kernel.org
> 
> Changes in v2:
> - switch to using ':' as path delimiter
> - Link to v1: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20260318-fw-path-v1-1-7884d9bf618f@kernel.org
> 
> ---
> Jeff Layton (2):
>       firmware_loader: add search_path= module option for multi-path firmware lookup
>       selftests/firmware: add search path test for firmware_class.search_path=
> 
>  drivers/base/firmware_loader/main.c           | 306 +++++++++++++++++++-------
>  tools/testing/selftests/firmware/Makefile     |   2 +-
>  tools/testing/selftests/firmware/fw_search.sh | 217 ++++++++++++++++++
>  3 files changed, 448 insertions(+), 77 deletions(-)
> ---
> base-commit: c369299895a591d96745d6492d4888259b004a9e
> change-id: 20260317-fw-path-a094c30259a5
> 
> Best regards,

I wrote these patches before the recent addition of the agentic
attribution guidelines. Both patches should get this tag as well:

    Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6

Cheers,
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH RFC] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

version_history.subject: [PATCH RFC] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes the SVC_POOL_* enum and mode selection heuristic, simplifying the pool map functions to always use the pernode path. The percpu mode is removed as it created excessive pools relative to the number of threads most deployments run. The module parameter and netlink interfaces are preserved for backward compatibility, but writing any value other than 'pernode' will return an error. The default pool mode is hardcoded to pernode, which provides one pool per node with proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever expressed concern that the patch hard-fails on legacy module parameter settings, instead recommending acceptance and ignoring of old values for backwards compatibility. He suggested changing the 'auto' mode to per-node and adding deprecation warnings before removal of other modes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Generally, I think the end goal of making "per-node" the default
and only setting is correct. My comments below are about how we
get there.

The main concern is not perturbing any configuration that today
happens to set the module parameter.

The proposed patch correctly preserves the shape of the user/kernel
interfaces (same module parameter name and perms, same netlink command
and attribute, same exported symbol names and signatures). The concern
is that the accepted input set has narrowed from four strings to one
and the setters reject the legacy three with -EINVAL. For the module
parameter that path runs at module load, so existing modprobe.d configs
written any time in the last ~19 years will cause load-time parameter
errors. Some might categorize that as a regression.
                                      
The commit message documents that non-"pernode" writes now return
-EINVAL. The historically correct approach for this kind of obsoleted
tuning knob is to accept-and-ignore the old values (plus the pr_notice)
rather than hard-fail.

Or, put another way, the proposed patch implements something slightly
different than true backwards compatibility. Userspace that previously
set pool_mode=global/percpu/auto now gets -EINVAL, which technically
speaking is a behavioral narrowing, not "backward compatibility."

I might go even further and suggest that perhaps for v7.2:

- Change the behavior of "auto" to be per-node
- Add a deprecation warning that is emitted when setting other modes,
  but don't warn when the value is specifically the only accepted one.

Then wait a few more cycles before removal of percpu and global.

Other notes:

o The existing pernode path assumes every online node has both CPUs and
  some memory. nr_online_nodes on some platforms (e.g., certain ARM64,
  CXL) counts memoryless or CPU-less nodes; for_each_node_with_cpus()
  vs. for_each_online_node() matters here. See
  svc_pool_map_init_pernode().

o Recommend review of some history on this topic:
  https://lore.kernel.org/linux-nfs/f027319a-378d-4b91-a418-c45218fb7a21@oracle.com/


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Chuck Lever's concern about the default pool_mode being 'global' instead of 'auto', explaining that 'pernode' is functionally identical to 'global' on single-NUMA hosts and 'percpu' mode is useless. Author suggests changing the default to 'pernode' initially as a compromise.

Author agrees that a warning is necessary when removing the percpu option, indicating a fix will be included in future revisions.

Author acknowledges that removing all pool modes except pernode is a significant change and agrees to stage it in slowly, but expresses concern about following up with later phases of deprecation.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's already the case if you're on a NUMA box. The problem is that
the default is not "auto" but "global". We could change that (easily),
but I'd argue that "auto" mode just devolves into "pernode" for two
reasons:

- "pernode" is functionally identical to "global" when there is only a
single NUMA node

- "percpu" mode is basically useless

If we want to "go slow" on this, then what I'd probably suggest is that
we just change the default to "pernode" initially.

Single node boxes that have this set to global today should see no real
change (functionally identical). Boxes with multiple NUMA nodes that
don't set it now, will start using pernode mode, but that's probably a
good thing in most cases.

---

We probably do still want to warn in that case. Once we remove the
option, the module load can fail, so we probably want to discourage
people from keeping the setting.

---

That's fair. We can stage this in slowly.

I'm terrible about following up with the later phases of long term
deprecation though. Suggestions on how to make sure we don't drop the
ball on finishing the change?

Re: [PATCH] NFSD: Put cache get-reqs dump attrs under reply

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 7/9 v2] mm/memcontrol: Make memory.low and memory.min tier-aware

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH 7/9 v2] mm/memcontrol: Make memory.low and memory.min tier-aware

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 9/9 v2] mm/memcontrol: Make memory.max tier-aware
series_items.patch_summary: This patch makes the memory.max limit tier-aware, allowing it to enforce a fair distribution of top-tier memory among workloads in isolated memory cgroups. The patch introduces selective reclaim on top-tier nodes based on the combination of limit breaches, ensuring consistent baseline performance by mitigating workload-external factors such as co-location and start order. The existing memory.max protection is extended to consider both total memory footprint and where that memory resides, enabling more efficient resource allocation in multi-workload environments.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 8/9 v2] mm/memcontrol: Make memory.high tier-aware
series_items.patch_summary: This patch makes the memory.high protection tier-aware, allowing it to enforce a fair distribution of toptier memory among workloads in isolated memory cgroups. It extends the existing memory.high protection by selectively reclaiming on toptier nodes when memory.high is breached or toptier.high is breached, and throttling allocations when toptier is breached. This ensures consistent baseline performance across workloads by considering workload-external factors such as co-location and start order.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 5/9 v2] mm/memcontrol: Set toptier limits proportional to memory limits
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the Linux kernel to set tiered limits proportional to memory limits when users write to memory limit sysfs files or when memory hotplug occurs. It introduces new read-only cgroup files to expose these derived limits, including `memory.toptier_{min,low,high,max}`. The changes are made in three files: `include/linux/memcontrol.h`, `mm/memcontrol.c`, and `mm/memory-tiers.c`. The patch aims to improve the dynamic adjustment of tiered memory limits based on system memory availability.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 4/9 v2] mm/memcontrol: charge/uncharge toptier memory to mem_cgroup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new page_counter called 'toptier' to mem_cgroup, which accounts for the usage of top-tier memory in parallel with existing memory accounting mechanisms. The goal is to capture the asymmetric utility of top-tier memory from lower-tier memory. Currently, this model only works on LRU folios and does not include enforcement mechanisms. Future patches will address other sites where top-tier memory should be accounted for.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 6/9 v2] mm/vmscan, memcontrol: Add nodemask to try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new nodemask parameter to the try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages function, allowing selective reclaim on specific nodes. The new function signature is intended for future use in patches that will selectively perform reclaim on top-tier memory and apply downward pressure when top-tier limits are breached but overall memory group-wide limits have not been reached. Currently, all callers pass NULL to the nodemask parameter, resulting in no functional changes with this patch.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 3/9 v2] mm/memcontrol: Refactor page_counter charging in try_charge_memcg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the page_counter charging in try_charge_memcg to make it easier to roll back partial charges when any one of the three page_counters fails to charge. The code is modified to handle different scenarios where memsw or memory counters fail to charge, allowing for more flexible and efficient handling of charging and uncharging operations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 2/9 v2] mm/memory-tiers: Introduce toptier utility functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces two utility functions, get_toptier_nodemask() and mt_scale_by_toptier(), to simplify the calculation of memory limits in tier-aware systems. The functions scale memory limits by the ratio of toptier capacity to total system capacity, ensuring that all cgroups are limited to their fair share of toptier memory. For single-node or single-tier systems, the scaling operation is a no-op. The patch also extends the existing node_is_toptier check to return a nodemask of all N_MEMORY nodes living on toptier.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 1/9 v2] cgroup: Introduce memory_tiered_limits cgroup mount option
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new cgroup mount option called memory_tiered_limits, which enables tier-proportional scaling of the memory cgroup controller limits. Currently, this option has no effect but will be used in later commits to scale memcg limits proportional to the system's toptier:total capacity ratio. The patch adds support for this new option by modifying several kernel files, including include/linux/cgroup-defs.h, include/linux/memcontrol.h, and kernel/cgroup/cgroup.c. The changes include adding a new flag to cgrp_dfl_root.flags and modifying the mem_cgroup_tiered_limits function to check if the memory_tiered_limits option is enabled.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/9 v2] mm/memcontrol: Make memory cgroup limits tier-aware
series_items.patch_summary: This series introduces tier-aware memory limits for the memory cgroup controller, allowing for proportional scaling of limits based on the system's toptier:total capacity ratio. The patch set includes changes to introduce a new mount option, modify existing limit enforcement code, and add tier-aware logic to reclaim memory when limits are breached.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the complexity of the tier-aware logic and suggested simplifying it. Also pointed out that the patch series does not address the issue of double-put bugs in fuse_try_move_page().

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Responded to David's concerns, explaining the reasoning behind the tier-aware logic and addressing the double-put bug issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
INTRODUCTION
============
Memory cgroups provide an interface that allow multiple works on a host to
co-exist via weak and strong memory isolation guarantees. This works, because
for the most part, all memory has equal utility. Isolating a cgroup\u2019s memory
footprint restricts how much it can hurt other workloads competing for memory,
or protects it from other cgroups looking for more memory.

However, on systems with tiered memory (e.g. CXL), memory utility is no longer
homogeneous; toptier and lowtier memory provide different performance
characteristics and have different scarcity, meaning memory footprint no longer
serves as an accurate representation of a cgroup\u2019s consumption of the system\u2019s
limited resources. As an extreme example, a cgroup with 10G of toptier
(e.g. DRAM) memory and a cgroup with 10G of lowtier (e.g. CXL) memory both
appear to be consuming the same amount of system resources from memcg\u2019s
perspective, despite the performance asymmetry between the two workloads.

Therefore on tiered systems, memory isolation cannot currently happen, as
workloads that are well-behaved within their memcg limits may still hurt the
performance of other well-behaving workloads by hogging more than its
\u201cfair share\u201d of toptier memory.

Introduce tier-aware memcg limits, which establish independent toptier limits
that scale with the memory limits and the ratio of toptier:total memory
available on the system.

INTERFACE
=========
This series introduces only one adjustable knob to userspace; a new cgroup mount
option \u201cmemory_tiered_limits\u201d which toggles whether the cgroup mount will scale
toptier limits. It also introduces 4 new read-only sysfs entries per-cgroup:
memory.toptier_{min, low, high, max}.

The new toptier memory limits are scaled according to the amount of toptier
memory and total memory available on the system as such:

memory.toptier_high = (toptier_mem / total_mem) * memory.high

For instance, on a host with 100GB memory, with 75G toptier and 25G CXL, the
\u201ctoptier ratio\u201d would be 75 / 100 = 0.75. A cgroup with the following memcg
limits {min: 8G, low: 12G, high: 20G, max: 24G} might see toptier limits scaled
at {min: 6G, low: 9G, high: 15G, max: 18G}.

USE CASES
=========
There are workloads that benefit from tiered memory limits, and those that do
not. Explicitly, hosts containing multiple workloads with the goal of maximizing
host-level throughput may see a regression because fairness is not free; it comes
at the cost of underutilized toptier memory, overhead to manage memory
migrations, and host-level memory hotness inversion.

On the other hand, fairness can prove to be a valuable resource for a number of
configurations, especially with workloads that want to raise the lower bound on
performance, rather than optimize for raw throughput:
- VM hosting services that must provide the maximal performance guarantee 
  (i.e. supremum) for any workload present on a host.
- Database workloads that want to minimize the maximum latency (i.e. infimum)
  for queries hosted on the host.
- Hosts running memory-isolated sharded workloads that blocks progress until the
  last shard terminates.
- Any workload that wants to minimize variance, as a means to gather measurable
  gains in performance over time.

TESTING
=======
To demonstrate the fairness and minimum performance guarantee increases, I
performed some performance tests across three data access patterns. All tests
were done on a 1T host with 750G DRAM and 250G CXL, spawning 4 220G workloads
{memory.high == memory.max == 220G}. 3 of those workloads are \u201cmemory hogs\u201d,
who get to run on the host and pre-allocate all of their memory. The last
workload is the \u201cvictim\u201d, who only gets to run once the other 3 workloads have
already allocated their memory. Once the victim allocates its memory as well,
we begin measuring read times for the following setups:
1. random memory access in the 220G anon region
2. hot / cold memory access, where the hot region (100G) gets 90% of the reads,
and the cold region (120G) gets 10% of the reads

First, let\u2019s look at what the results look like with NUMAB=2:

Per-cgroup throughput (Mops/s):
Cgroup     Baseline   Tier-Aware
------     --------   ----------
hog          21.457       17.733
hog          22.773       16.329
hog          22.630       16.549
victim       12.315       16.950

DRAM / CXL distribution (GB):
Cgroup          Baseline              Tier-Aware
------          --------              ----------
hog      220.0 DRAM / 0.0 CXL    181.6 DRAM / 38.4 CXL
hog      220.0 DRAM / 0.0 CXL    181.6 DRAM / 38.4 CXL
hog      220.0 DRAM / 0.0 CXL    181.6 DRAM / 38.4 CXL
victim   69.3 DRAM  / 150.7 CXL  186.7 DRAM / 33.3 CXL

Experiment 2 (hot / cold access)

Per-cgroup throughput (Mops/s):
Cgroup     Baseline   Tier-Aware
------     --------   ----------
wl0          24.280       17.815
wl1          23.929       15.019
wl2          23.645       15.605
wl3          11.624       15.998

DRAM / CXL distribution (GB):
Cgroup          Baseline               Tier-Aware
------          --------               ----------
wl0      220.0 DRAM / 0.0 CXL    181.6 DRAM / 38.4 CXL
wl1      220.0 DRAM / 0.0 CXL    181.6 DRAM / 38.4 CXL
wl2      220.0 DRAM / 0.0 CXL    181.6 DRAM / 38.4 CXL
wl3      70.4 DRAM  / 149.6 CXL  186.7 DRAM / 33.3 CXL

With NUMAB=0, the pattern remains the same, but overall, throughput seems
increased, and variance seems decreased.
I believe there is a negative interaction here between NUMA balancing\u2019s
host-level hotness tracking, and the tier-aware memcg limit\u2019s push to make
memcg-aware migration decisions (see open questions below).

The results above demonstrate the desired effect of fairly distributing CXL
usage across the workloads regardless of when they were launched, and minimizing
performance variance.

OPEN QUESTIONS (for mailing list & for LSFMMBPF)
================================================
1. Should memory.toptier_max be enforced? And if so, what should it look like?
   In my testing, I have found that enforcing memory.toptier_max in the same way
   as memory.max leads to significant throttling, as each allocation above the
   toptier limit causes a loop of allocate on toptier --> scan toptier LRU for
   victim --> demote victim page --> allocate on toptier...
   Thus, in my test above, I ran with the last patch (memory.toptier_max
   enforcement) disabled. Are there use-cases for enforcing memory.toptier_max?
   For this RFC, I\u2019ve included it for review, but I feel that it makes sense to
   drop toptier enforcement.
2. This version of the code does its best to generalize the memcg stock system
   as much as possible, but still only makes a distinction between toptier /
   lowtier. Does it make sense to support 3+ tiers? Are there currently real
   systems / hardware out there that desires to enforce fairness at that scale?
2-1. Should swap be considered its own tier?
3. Should users be able to tune anything? Currently, the only choice is for
   users to enable the limits or not. Options for userspace tuning include:
   setting cgroup-wide toptier limits; system-wide toptier:lowtier ratios;
   cgroup-level toptier:lowtier ratios.
4. Tiered memcg limits interfere with existing promotion mechanisms like NUMA
   balancing (NUMAB2), that promote memory on a systemwide basis, ignoring
   process and memcg contexts. What kinds of promotion mechanisms should be used
   to work in memcg-aware contexts?

DEPENDENCIES
============
This work is built upon my recent RFC [1] to move stocks from the memcg level to
the page_counter level, to make the toptier charging path cheaper. In addition,
this patch is limited to working on LRU folios; kmem memory and memory that is
otherwise not charged on an lruvec-basis (i.e. has both physical node & memcg
information; aka enum memcg_stat_item) is not accounted for. There are landed &
ongoing efforts to introduce per-lruvec accounting for these as well:
- vmalloc (from Johannes): mm-stable [2]
- zswap / zswapped / zswap_incompressible [3]
- percpu: in progress [4]

CHANGELOG V1 --> V2
===================
- The toptier:total ratio calculation has been simplified to ignore cpusets and
  now exist as a system-wide ratio. This came from the realization that having
  cgroups that opt-in and opt-out of CXL co-existing on the system leads to a
  question on how the limits should be enforced, and whether such a configuration
  is even desirable.
- The simplification above means struct page_counter can be per-memcg, not
  mem_cgroup_per_node.
- Independent memcg stock management for toptier
- Included min / max enforcement (for max, see questions above)
- Exported toptier limits as read-only sysfs files
- Turned the build config into a mount option, as suggested by Michal Hocko

Thank you for reading this long cover letter. Have a great day everyone!

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260410210742.550489-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260220191035.3703800-1-hannes@cmpxchg.org/
[3] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260226192936.3190275-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/
[4] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260404033844.1892595-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/


Joshua Hahn (9):
  cgroup: Introduce memory_tiered_limits cgroup mount option
  mm/memory-tiers: Introduce toptier utility functions
  mm/memcontrol: Refactor page_counter charging in try_charge_memcg
  mm/memcontrol: charge/uncharge toptier memory to mem_cgroup
  mm/memcontrol: Set toptier limits proportional to memory limits
  mm/vmscan, memcontrol: Add nodemask to try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages
  mm/memcontrol: Make memory.low and memory.min tier-aware
  mm/memcontrol: Make memory.high tier-aware
  mm/memcontrol: Make memory.max tier-aware

 include/linux/cgroup-defs.h  |   5 +
 include/linux/memcontrol.h   |  35 ++++
 include/linux/memory-tiers.h |  17 ++
 include/linux/swap.h         |   3 +-
 kernel/cgroup/cgroup.c       |  12 ++
 mm/memcontrol-v1.c           |   6 +-
 mm/memcontrol.c              | 306 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++----
 mm/memory-tiers.c            |  46 +++++-
 mm/vmscan.c                  |  11 +-
 9 files changed, 402 insertions(+), 39 deletions(-)

-- 
2.52.0

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch makes the memory.low and memory.min protection mechanisms in Linux aware of memory tiers, allowing for fair distribution of toptier memory among workloads on machines serving multiple isolated workloads. This addresses a problem where enforcing proportional best-effort and guaranteed memory protection of toptier memory was not possible due to lack of tier-awareness. The patch extends the existing protection mechanisms by adding checks for toptier memory usage, ensuring that each workload's performance is less impacted by external factors such as co-location with other workloads. This approach enables consistent and baseline performance across workloads on multi-workload hosts.
